rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical C-H halogenation (chlorination and bromination) of electron-deficient arenes/heterocycles (quinones, coumarins, quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones, 1,3-diketones) using HX as halide source in an undivided cell
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of benzoquinone to hydroquinone, and anodic oxidation of chloride to Cl2 (electrophilic chlorinating species); after SEAr on hydroquinone, anodic reoxidation regenerates the quinone product

key_intermediates.name: molecular chlorine (Cl2) generated by anodic oxidation of chloride

electron_flow: Cathode reduces the quinone (with H+) to the hydroquinone while the anode oxidizes Cl- to Cl2; Cl2 electrophilically chlorinates the electron-rich hydroquinone, and the chlorinated hydroquinone is then reoxidized at the anode to deliver the halogenated quinone product.

probe_reagents.name: BHT (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction completely suppressed; BHT-Cl adduct 3a detected by HRMS, but authors interpret results as inconsistent with a free-radical pathway

probe_reagents.name: N,N-diallyl-4-methylbenzenesulfonamide
probe_reagents.observation: Gave dichlorinated open-chain product 3b in 54% yield with no cyclization, arguing against a radical addition pathway and supporting an electrophilic (ionic) chlorination

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry
probe_reagents.observation: CV analysis supports cathodic reduction of quinone and anodic oxidation of chloride, consistent with the proposed paired mechanism

notes: Although BHT shut down the reaction and a BHT-Cl adduct was detected (which could suggest radical involvement), the lack of cyclization in the diallylsulfonamide probe led the authors to favor an electrophilic (ionic) chlorination by anodically generated Cl2 on a cathodically generated hydroquinone, rather than a free-radical chain. A no-current control gave no reaction, confirming the electrochemical requirement.
